text: Hello everyone, this is Roxen Liu back with a demonstration of image inversion. I'm going to show two cases where I find that image inversion really helps with facilitating the defect closure.
The surgeon performs an ETEP procedure with a right-sided approach, revealing a visible suprapubic defect.
The surgeon's right hand approaches the suprapubic defect at an almost perpendicular angle, which may complicate the suturing process.
The surgeon's left hand maneuvers with ample space, approaching the defect more parallel compared to the right hand, which faces difficulty due to its perpendicular angle.
The surgeon swaps the positions of the left and right instruments, followed by rotating the camera to adjust the view for better access to the surgical site.

manually and right now it's looking at the wrong location so I'm going to use the touchpad to flip the image. I've also reassigned the instruments on the touchpad so that if you look at the bottom screen, two and four are flipped.
The surgeon uses their right hand, previously the left hand, to suture the suprapubic defect with improved ease due to their right-handedness.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the suturing instrument to improve access for suturing, aiming for a technique similar to closing a laparotomy incision.
It's very natural, very intuitive. And I think it really speeds up suturing.

If I were to try to close this defect right side up, I would be struggling and my suture line would not be as clean. I think my bites would not be as precise and I would have trouble taking bites of that.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to take precise bites of both the fascia and the hernia sac, demonstrating careful manipulation of the tissue.

So I use this technique quite often when I have a either suprapubic or ventral hernia, I'm sorry, suprapubic or subxiphoid defect when I'm lateral docked. So this scenario comes up quite often with these long midline labrodomy defects.

The surgeon is docked on the patient's right side, addressing a lower abdominal defect similar to previous scenarios.

In the inverted image view, the anterior rectus sheath on the left side of the screen is retracted laterally, exposing the rectus muscle. The surgeon peels back the rectus muscle to grasp the anterior sheath more easily.
